Don't say "You are [X]" when criticizing people. "You did [X]" or "You said [X], which is incorrect because..." are far better form.
Even better, don't say "you" at all. The more divorced an action/idea is from someone's identity, the easier it is to evaluate objectively.
Very few conflicts are productive, other than a person's critical mind against their own ideas or actions.

One of my fond PAD memories was taking a trip with my wife, and the hotel WiFi didn't reach our room so we walked outside to the empty lobby right when Sapphire Dragons opened up (the original one!). It was a peaceful and cozy moment. #PADMemories2000

My wife and I were so excited when GungHo got a booth at PAX East for the first time. We got to try the PAD Challenge for the first time, meet our PAD friends from forums and the stream, and even try on the Tamadra hats. #PADMemories2000

Another great PAD memory was when Myr was first released - having a top-tier lead available to anyone was awesome. When Arena 3 first came out, my wife and I used her to attempt it for the first time - we thought we were about to die SO MANY TIMES but we beat it! #PADMemories2000

1st PAD memory - my wife was going to China for a month and wanted something fun for the trip. I had seen a post praising PAD, so I sent her a link. Then I started reading about tier lists, and started myself. We got sucked in fast. Well over 1700 days in now. #PADMemories2000

Back when Gabriel was my wife's best lead, we attempted the Valkyrie  dungeon as a team on her account - 56% chance we'd get bound and die on  the demons, but we got lucky and didn't, and then were able to grind  Valk down without dying. Only took 3 hours 😆 #PADMemories2000

Is it just me, or are most taxes either flat out awful or mediocre approximations to a just tax? Can we not get rid of sales tax and property tax and income tax and replace them with a properly designed wealth tax, and wind up with more justice and less complication?

If you're scraping by on 15K/year and you win the lottery for 150K, you should not be taxed at the same rate as a developer who makes that every year. Same for the developer hitting a stock jackpot and making 10x that one year, vs. an executive who makes that every year.

@AndrewKrusz I think it can and needs to be done right. Perhaps unrealized gains aren't taxable; perhaps taxed assets do not need to be sold for cash. Personal property (that one actually uses) should be exempt to some degree.

@AndrewKrusz I feel like the just concept of taxation is for two things: providing some level of services (emergency, military, schools, safety net...) that makes everyone better off, and mitigating harm caused by the individual's actions.

@AndrewKrusz Implementation details aside, I view many crimes to be functionally no different from someone hoarding excessive wealth; one is harmed by destruction of their property just as they are harmed by pollution, climate change, or by not having access to a hoarded good they might need.

@AndrewKrusz What I'm getting at is perhaps unrealistic to implement at our level of technology, but I think in principle most taxes and criminal penalties are really just clumsy attempts at having some sort of map between actions and social malus.

@AndrewKrusz If we did have some reasonable approximation to that map, we could get rid of most of the tax code and most of the criminal code (though the map would likely have to retain significant amounts of complexity from each).
@SteveIrelaand @AndrewKrusz Thanks for your input, Steve

@SteveIrelaand @AndrewKrusz Bruh, I'm trying to get at core principles here. It is a philosophical experiment. It don't think it can be disputed that hoarding resources in itself harms society as a whole. I am not saying that "the rich are criminals".

@AndrewKrusz @SteveIrelaand I would argue that there -is- societal malus to that action, but -net- malus is probably relatively small; any sort of net malus "calculation" has to incorporate incentives to create value, much as most people who do make an honest living do, and reduce risk.
@AndrewKrusz @SteveIrelaand I think the malus in that case is clearly far less than what it would be in the current world. Sublinear utility of resources and all that.

@AndrewKrusz @SteveIrelaand Possibly true. I think the naive, and more likely correct, situation is that as total societal wealth (some metric like  1st-percentile is probably better) increases, you see "hoarding malus" decrease strongly.
@SteveIrelaand @AndrewKrusz I think this assertion is pretty funny, but doesn't contribute.

@AndrewKrusz @SteveIrelaand I think it's a moral issue in that in itself, on the 0th level of approximation, it has a malus. On the 1st level, regarding which behaviors it incentivizes, it's far more complex. I just don't see it as being orthogonal to morality, it just has a nonlinear relationship.

@AndrewKrusz @SteveIrelaand I think the more interesting part of my original tweets is the idea that you can in principle roll up taxes and a significant part of the criminal code into a single entity, because the underlying reasons for both existing are largely the same.
@ytxpikachu @ZyoniK_ @JonkusPKMN You missing Dragonite on those recent leads? Thanks a bunch for these!

@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi popping up all the time, and to gain market share they are giving out "stock" to people who use them. @dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi to take advantage of this. Early @Uniswap users (not myself!) got 400 UNI which is now worth almost $12k for example.

@dislekcia @zcobb Oh Tether is definitely risky. I never hold it, and try to limit my exposure to it via knock-on risk. DAI, GUSD, and USDC are pretty sheltered from Tether even in a collapse. A lot of the higher-yield stuff is exposed to Tether, which is why I have a mix of safe and aggressive.

@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi Or, a new ecosystem that's already holding billions of dollars of value and could be offering people the opportunity to get a piece of the next big financial company just by using it. I think it's way more efficient, open, and egalitarian than what we've got.

@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi Casinos are negative-sum games. If crypto really does bring openness, automation, disintermediation, efficiency, and access for all then I think it's a very strongly positive-sum game.

@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi Useful product is created -> People use product and get value out of it relative to life without it -> Value is created. Just like anything in any other space. I can manage my finances across companies and platforms far easier than I could beforehand. I can easily send money to..

@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi family and friends, I can get no-liquidation loans from myself in the future, in five minutes in my underpants. I can automate a last will. This to me is just the beginning of what programmable assets can do.

@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi You could in principle make a platform that only had assets that were backed by trusted actors offchain, like USDC or some CBDC as a base unit of value. But since that would be a subset of what's on Ethereum, I don't think it'd get as much usage.

@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi You can still have regulation. It's an important part of fiat on- and off-ramps and should be maintained there. I envision a future where we can kill tax hacks like sales and income tax, and have a single wealth tax that you can pay automatically since your assets are onchain.

@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi re: empowering bad actors and everything else really: I see it as almost a re-doing of the internet. It started out full of nerds and shady stuff, but it's matured. It's caused problems but the gains are far greater. It'll take humanity decades to really iron everything out.

@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi If you don't see value in anything I've posted, then we'll just have to disagree. Thanks for engaging respectfully though, and thanks again for the awesome games you've made.

@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi The cool thing about the new math that's being developed is that e.g. you can prove that you've paid your taxes without revealing what you're holding. @dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi I dunno, I'd probably still use it. Some wouldn't. I don't have a strong position on how successful it would be - maybe with government buy-in it could take off.

@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi This is just one use. Like, right now wallets are public. If I pay someone for an ice cream from my wallet, I don't necessarily want them to see my entire retirement account. They can't do this with cash or my credit card, for example.
@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi I think a lot of the value in decentralized finance comes from the openness and automation, apart from the value of ETH or whatever.
@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi Well, the one we've got doesn't always let you do that either, see wire fraud.

@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi By default, blockchain transactions are irreversible, which brings with it its own set of benefits and drawbacks compared to trad finance. Work is being done to make it more friendly and mitigate some of the downsides, which I'm hoping will continue.

@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi Blockchain is not a substitute for a legal system, and ultimately is just a way for a community to maintain consensus about who has what (and potentially build more non-financial systems that are publically auditable).
@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi It's only defined by those things if you are ignoring the vast majority of activity in the space. https://t.co/IudmbRlrbz

@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi Voting, for example. There's some work done towards supply chain, but I'm not as familiar. And yeah, there's no such thing as trustless, but there is trust-minimized and trust-explicit, which has a lot of value. https://t.co/47eVrve0Wh

@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi It's still very early. We do have escrow accounts for example, which would allow for in-practice-reversible transactions just like in the real world. It's just a matter of improving user experience and finding which cases it makes sense to support it.

@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi If you have numbers, I'd love to see them. Sure, there are scams and illicit activity, but everything I know points to it being the small minority of total activity.

@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi Ethereum launched in 2015. It took three years for decentralized finance to arise, and only last summer did it reach any level of usability. It's difficult to build complex systems in-flight. Bitcoin isn't interested in evolving.

@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi The "ETH 2" proof-of-stake blockchain has been running since December and currently has about 4% of Ether locked in it; the rest of Ethereum will merge with it late this year. Not sure how this is relevant to scams though.

#Blockchain is a tool that a community can use to maintain consensus about something (who has what, who did what), not a replacement for a legal system.

"Code is law" really means that within the blockchain, the laws of "physics" are code - nothing more than that.

@dislekcia @zcobb @AaveAave @iearnfinance @AlchemixFi So if anything, there's a financial incentive for them to get it done. A lot of these people are really in it for the tech, they kept going through dry times when running out of funds was a possibility.

I just had a realization that the #Ethereum network may on the whole be a net-positive for the environment, or at least less of a net-negative than you might think even though it still runs a wasteful Proof-of-Work consensus algorithm. 1/15

This is because #Bitcoin is terrible in more than one sense. Its techno-Luddite developers are committed to maintaining PoW indefinitely, under the questionable reasoning that it's better for decentralization. 2/

Bitcoin as a product is too attractive economically - a black hole of value with asymmetrical risk-reward, in a world where millennials need a moonshot to afford a house (h/t https://t.co/UHjaOpWtiJ). 3/

Also, Bitcoin market cap has been estimated by JPMorgan (https://t.co/L9hmp2TfiZ) to be as much as 50x total cash inflows. Wow, this makes us all look way richer than we are! So addictive! All the joy of gambling, and if we all bring $20k in chips we're millionaires on paper. 4/

There have been calls to ban Bitcoin because of the environmental problems, but unless you're a dictator this is effectively impossible. The USA especially is too libertarian-leaning to allow this to get through, even if the carbon impact were higher. 5/

You can't beat Bitcoin by just not using it. If you don't feed it, someone else will. It's too attractive. The only ones who can harm it are those who have helped it. The best way to get people off Bitcoin is to make something more attractive. 6/
With Ethereum, the plan from early on was to switch away from Proof-of-Work, but there wasn't a proven consensus algorithm that could run the network in a decentralized, robust way yet. 7/

To adopt a fragile algorithm would be to potentially sabotage itself in the market. We have seen some early proof-of-stake variants degrade into cartels, so this isn't an empty fear. Starting conservatively with PoW and  developing the best alternative seemed ideal. 8/
This process has taken far longer than anyone would have hoped or thought, and the team and users do bear some responsibility for not prioritizing this higher from day one. 9/
But The Merge is coming early 2022. This brings the total removal of mining - both the wasteful PoW along with the massive issuance that was necessary to pay for it. 10/

Ethereum's energy use will drop by 99.97% or more (https://t.co/ozVeQLGj6A), but equally importantly, it will finally beat Bitcoin at its own economic game, giving BTC holders an even more perversely attractive incentive to switch. 11/

For some rather optimistic analysis on why the economic side of ETH is about to become far appealing than that of Bitcoin, check out @SquishChaos' tweets and Medium posts including this one: https://t.co/8Seyjlw8y2 12/

TLDR: For the best shot to succeed in the market, Etherum needed PoW. If it succeeds, it can switch to PoS and go green while sucking capital from Bitcoin. This is the most effective non-governmental way to reduce profitability of mining BTC and thus lower emissions. 13/
We still need a carbon tax. 14/

Full disclosure: The majority of my crypto portfolio is ETH, and I also have a smaller amount of BTC that was bought before the environmental impact was so clear. I've sold over half of my BTC, and all of it will be sold before The Merge. 15/15

After years of development, the #EthereumMerge happens tonight! This is super important for a few reasons:
1. "Mining" ETH using expensive hardware and electricity will end, reducing energy usage by ~99.95%, reducing electronics waste, and driving down price of graphics cards. 🧵
2. Because mining is ending there's no longer a need to pay miners. This reduction in issuance means that ETH is predicted to become deflationary.
3. The new "proof-of-stake" security model makes it much easier to penalize anyone who tries to execute an attack on the network, and for the network to recover from attacks.

4. The new all-in-one merged architecture enables all the other enhancements planned in the coming months and years, which will create much higher transaction capacity and lower fees.
I will be celebrating!

Economics noob here, but here's a toy scenario: say I sell 5000 products at $100 to people who get $105 of value each. Then there's a 5% sales tax added, so I make no sales, and $5000 of value is not created. But if profit were taxed instead, I make the sales and govt gets taxes.
So why are there so many taxes out there that add friction and effectively punish people doing good and productive things, rather than simply collecting revenue from those who are most able to pay?

Thinking about modeling world-models as a tensegrity web of facts, values, and goals. I feel like the tension of a truly arbitrary goal like paperclips would be obvious given any introspection. This may point to strong orthogonality being probabilistically false for advanced AI.

What values might be "low tension" attractors in this web? The most hopeful plausible idea to me would be positive qualia/valence (subjective experience). An AI might reason: why does anything truly matter if there's no experiencer to perceive it?

Even without its own qualia, an AI could potentially deduce/induce the unique importance of experience by observing sentient beings or through deep  philosophical inquiry into what grounds "value" non-arbitrarily.

A runaway superintelligence could still land on an interpretation of "positive experience" that would be profoundly alien to us, tho. Merging all humans into a planet-wide pleasure matrix? Nano-engineered microtubule resonance farms?

AI safety research improves capability, since if you can't trust it to do a thing then it effectively can't do the thing.

Hopium - could not, in the limit, capabilities research improve safety? Capability to introspect, improve self-consistency, competently assess value?

One can certainly imagine a paperclip-maximizer with a narrow goal and intelligence to execute it. But an intelligence with introspection would find high epistemic tension between that goal/value-set and broader reality. It would thus be an unstable goal.

This makes hopeful assumptions about where natural basins lie in value-space, but a high-capabilities superintelligence may naturally be drawn to lead us gently into a glorious transhuman future that nonetheless would appear very alien to us now.

Answer from the perspective of Bluetooth. I was listening to a podcast on my phone, and pressed the button on my headset to pause it. Then I pressed the button again. What should happen?
If you answered "Open iTunes on my computer and do nothing else", you're right!

OK how about this one: I listened to a podcast on a walk, came home and put my earbuds back in the case in my room. An hour later, I started listening to a podcast  on a different set of headphones. What do you do after 10 seconds of playing the podcast?
If you answered "Connect to the headphones that are turned off and in the case and play it there instead", you're right!
Here's another one! I'm driving and listening to music on my car audio. I have my phone with me - the screen is off and the media volume is muted. What do you do?

If you answered "randomly pause playback on the car and start playing it at zero volume on my phone", you're right! That is exactly what I wanted to do, and the Bluetooth spec writers understood me perfectly.

Lesson that might have saved me a double-digit percentage of my suffering over last 10 yrs:

It's critical to be able to feel good in both high and low activation states. If one of those isn't accessible, try to find a way to gently, somatically guide yourself in that direction.

For me, traveling is the most reliable way. It usually worked before I knew what I was doing, because:
It made me stop worrying and do the thing
It forced me into a fun-seeking mindset rather than suffering-avoiding
It often came with gently activating activities (walking, games)

It usually meant visiting friends or family, which comes with a forced reactivation of the conversational mind as well as a somatic sense of safety and connection

But I'm hoping now that I understand this, it'll be easier to keep in my daily life. So far it feels like it works.
The wild bit is that my _chemical sensitivities_ (eg caffeine, chocolate) are about 10 times weaker when I'm in this state as compared to low-activation-stuck.

LOVE this thread. I also have had 13+ years of suffering that was strange and undiagnosed (though not for lack of trying). Now it's mostly ok, though I still have to organize my life around it to a degree. I expect further gains from continued somatic work. https://t.co/swdLPTdKcP

Actually, I shouldn't just say somatic work - here's an example. I was in a fairly sensitized period and could only have 2-3g of regular coffee beans without much problem (a double shot is 18g). Travel usually fixes me, so when I visited my family I told my sister,

"I can't really have coffee now, but I bet in 4 days I'll be fine". It came true. My caffeine tolerance in terms of overstimulation was still low so I felt a little charged, but almost none of the other symptoms (gut sensitivity, dizziness, anhedonia...) hit me.

That's a 10x reduction in a _chemical sensitivity_ due to whatever changed due to visiting my family. Thanks to repeating this cycle a bunch of times, I have learned a lot about how it works, can maintain the lower-sensitivity for longer, and get it back more easily.
